teachingSummary:
**Core Rule — The Expectation Interest:** The overarching principle of contractual damages is to put the claimant in the position they would have been in had the contract been performed. This is the **expectation interest** (or *loss of bargain*). The classic formulation comes from Parke B in *Robinson v Harman* (1848) 1 Ex 850: 'The rule of the common law is, that where a party sustains a loss by reason of a breach of contract, he is, so far as money can do it, to be placed in the same situation, with respect to damages, as if the contract had been performed.'

**Loss of Bargain:** The claimant recovers the net value of what was promised but not delivered. For example, if a seller fails to deliver goods worth £10,000 that the buyer contracted to buy for £8,000, the buyer's expectation loss is £2,000 — the difference between contract price and market value. The claimant should not be worse off than if the contract had been performed.

**Cost of Cure vs Diminution in Value:** In *Ruxley Electronics and Construction Ltd v Forsyth* [1996] AC 344, a swimming pool was built 6 inches too shallow (7ft 6in instead of 7ft 9in). The cost of rebuilding was £21,560, but the diminution in market value was nil (the pool's value was unaffected). The House of Lords refused the cost of cure — it was unreasonable and disproportionate. Instead, the claimant was awarded £2,500 for **loss of amenity** — the personal, non-pecuniary loss of not having the pool he wanted. Lord Mustill and Lord Lloyd emphasised that cost of cure is not automatic; it must be reasonable and proportionate to the benefit.

**In short:** Expectation damages put the claimant where they would have been; the cost of cure is available only where it is reasonable and proportionate. *Ruxley* shows that sometimes only loss of amenity may be awarded.
oscoaReferences: Robinson v Harman (1848) 1 Ex 850
Ruxley Electronics and Construction Ltd v Forsyth [1996] AC 344

question: In __________ v Harman (1848) 1 Ex 850, Parke B formulated the classic rule: contractual damages place the claimant in the position they would have been in had the contract been performed.
feedback: Correct. *Robinson v Harman* (1848) 1 Ex 850 — the claimant was a tenant who lost a profitable lease because the landlord refused to grant it. The court awarded expectation damages: the profit the tenant would have made had the lease been granted. Parke B's statement remains foundational.
oscoaReferences: Robinson v Harman (1848) 1 Ex 850

question: The __________ interest is the primary measure of contractual damages: the claimant is entitled to be placed in the same position as if the contract had been performed, so far as money can do it.
feedback: Correct. The expectation interest puts the claimant where they would have been with full performance. *Robinson v Harman* (1848) 1 Ex 850. Compare the reliance interest (putting the claimant back to pre-contract position) and the restitution interest (disgorging the defendant's gains).
oscoaReferences: Robinson v Harman (1848) 1 Ex 850

question: A builder contracts to build a house extension for £50,000. The builder abandons the work after spending £20,000. At that point, the work is worth £15,000 to the homeowner and another builder will charge £40,000 to complete it. What is the homeowner's expectation loss under *Robinson v Harman* (1848)?

options.text: £50,000 — the full contract price
options.feedback: Incorrect. The full contract price would overcompensate — the homeowner would have had to pay that anyway. Expectation damages are the *net* loss.

options.text: £15,000 — the value of the work done so far
options.feedback: Incorrect. That measures the benefit received, not the loss. Expectation damages look at the cost to complete and the net position.

options.text: £10,000 — the extra cost to complete (£40,000) minus the unpaid balance under the original contract (£30,000), giving the net loss
options.feedback: Correct. The homeowner contracted to pay £50,000 total. £20,000 already spent by builder. Remaining owed: £30,000. Completion now costs £40,000. Net loss = £40,000 - £30,000 = £10,000. This puts the homeowner in the position they would have been in: a completed extension for a net outlay of £50,000. *Robinson v Harman* (1848) 1 Ex 850.

options.text: £5,000 — the difference between the price paid and the value received
options.feedback: Incorrect. This misunderstands expectation loss. The homeowner has not yet paid the full £50,000. The correct analysis compares cost-to-complete with the unpaid contract balance.

feedback: The expectation loss calculation: the homeowner should end up with the extension for a total outlay of £50,000. If they pay the new builder £40,000, total outlay = £40,000 + £20,000 = £60,000. To be put in the position as if the contract was performed (total cost £50,000), they need £10,000 in damages. *Robinson v Harman* (1848) 1 Ex 850.
oscoaReferences: Robinson v Harman (1848) 1 Ex 850

question: Match each damages measure to its correct description:
answer: Expectation interest=Put the claimant in the position as if the contract had been performed; Reliance interest=Put the claimant back to the position before the contract was made; Restitution interest=Strip the defendant of gains made at the claimant's expense; Loss of amenity=Compensation for non-pecuniary loss (Ruxley v Forsyth)

options.text: Put the claimant in the position as if the contract had been performed
Put the claimant back to the position before the contract was made
Strip the defendant of gains made at the claimant's expense
Compensation for non-pecuniary loss (Ruxley v Forsyth)

feedback: Correct. The expectation interest is the primary contractual measure. Reliance is an alternative where expectation loss is too speculative. Restitution is exceptional. Loss of amenity, recognised in *Ruxley* [1996], compensates for the personal, non-financial loss of not getting what was contracted for.
oscoaReferences: Robinson v Harman (1848) 1 Ex 850
Ruxley Electronics and Construction Ltd v Forsyth [1996] AC 344

question: A homeowner contracts for a swimming pool to be built 7ft 9in deep. The pool is built 7ft 6in deep — safe for diving but 3 inches shallower than specified. The cost to rebuild to the correct depth is £20,000. The pool's market value is unaffected by the 3-inch difference. The homeowner wants £20,000. Applying *Ruxley v Forsyth* [1996]:

options.text: The homeowner gets £20,000 — the cost of cure is always available for breach of contract
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Ruxley* [1996] established that cost of cure is not automatic — it must be reasonable and proportionate to the benefit obtained.

options.text: The homeowner gets nothing — if the market value is unaffected, there is no loss at all
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Ruxley* [1996] rejected the 'all or nothing' approach. Even where diminution in value is nil, the homeowner has suffered a personal, non-pecuniary loss — loss of amenity.

options.text: The homeowner gets the difference between the contract price and the market value of the defective pool
options.feedback: Incorrect. That would be diminution in value — which is zero. *Ruxley* [1996] recognises that this measure can undercompensate the claimant.

options.text: The homeowner gets a modest sum for loss of amenity — the cost of cure is disproportionate (£20,000 for 3 inches), and damages for the personal, non-financial loss are appropriate
options.feedback: Correct. *Ruxley Electronics and Construction Ltd v Forsyth* [1996] AC 344 is directly on point. The House of Lords held: (1) cost of cure was unreasonable and disproportionate; (2) diminution in value was nil; (3) but the claimant suffered a real loss — loss of amenity — and was awarded £2,500.

feedback: This mirrors *Ruxley Electronics and Construction Ltd v Forsyth* [1996] AC 344 almost exactly. The House of Lords established three important principles: (1) the cost of cure is not an automatic measure — it must be reasonable and proportionate; (2) diminution in value may undercompensate (here, nil); (3) loss of amenity is a valid head of non-pecuniary loss in contract, recognising that a claimant may suffer real personal loss even when market value is unaffected.
oscoaReferences: Ruxley Electronics and Construction Ltd v Forsyth [1996] AC 344

question: Which TWO of the following correctly describe the expectation interest under *Robinson v Harman* (1848)?

options.text: The claimant is entitled to be placed in the position as if the contract had been performed, so far as money can do it
options.feedback: Correct. Parke B's formulation in *Robinson v Harman* (1848) 1 Ex 850: 'where a party sustains a loss by reason of a breach of contract, he is, so far as money can do it, to be placed in the same situation… as if the contract had been performed.'

options.text: The claimant is entitled only to the return of any money paid under the contract
options.feedback: Incorrect. That is the reliance interest (or restitution), not the expectation interest. Expectation puts the claimant forward — to where they would have been post-performance.

options.text: Expectation damages include lost profits that the claimant would have made had the contract been performed
options.feedback: Correct. Loss of bargain includes the profit the claimant would have earned. In *Robinson v Harman* (1848) itself, the tenant recovered the profit he would have made from a lease the landlord refused to grant.

options.text: The expectation interest is only available where the defendant acted fraudulently
options.feedback: Incorrect. The expectation interest is the default measure in contract, regardless of the defendant's state of mind. Contract damages are strict — fault is not required.

options.text: Expectation damages are limited to the contract price and cannot exceed it
options.feedback: Incorrect. Expectation damages can far exceed the contract price — e.g., lost profits on a particularly lucrative deal. The contract price is just one factor in the calculation.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (c). The expectation interest is the primary contractual measure — it looks forward to where the claimant would have been after performance. Lost profits are a core component. *Robinson v Harman* (1848) 1 Ex 850 is the foundational authority.
oscoaReferences: Robinson v Harman (1848) 1 Ex 850

question:
Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'Under English law, the measure of damages for breach of contract is always the cost of putting right the defective performance. If a builder builds a wall 2mm out of alignment, the claimant can recover the full cost of demolishing and rebuilding the entire wall, regardless of cost or proportionality.'
answer: Cost of cure is not automatic — it must be reasonable and proportionate. *Ruxley Electronics v Forsyth* [1996] AC 344: where the cost of cure is disproportionate to the benefit obtained, the court will award diminution in value or loss of amenity instead. Demolishing a wall for 2mm misalignment would be grossly disproportionate.
feedback: Found it. The House of Lords in *Ruxley Electronics and Construction Ltd v Forsyth* [1996] AC 344 rejected the argument that cost of cure is the automatic measure. A claimant cannot insist on an unreasonable remedy. Where cost of cure is disproportionate, the court will instead award diminution in value (if any) or loss of amenity. A 2mm misalignment, causing no functional or aesthetic harm, would likely result in nominal or loss-of-amenity damages only.
oscoaReferences: Ruxley Electronics and Construction Ltd v Forsyth [1996] AC 344

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule — Reliance Interest:** A claimant may, as an alternative to expectation damages, claim **reliance damages** — putting the claimant back in the position they were in *before* the contract was made. This is also called **wasted expenditure**. It covers expenses incurred in preparing to perform or in reliance on the contract, rendered futile by the defendant's breach. The claimant cannot claim both expectation and reliance damages — they must elect.

**Anglia Television Ltd v Reed [1972] 1 QB 60:** Robert Reed, an actor, contracted to appear in a TV film but repudiated the contract before filming began. Anglia could not find a replacement and abandoned the project. They could not prove what profit the film would have made (the expectation interest was too speculative). The Court of Appeal awarded reliance damages: Anglia recovered all wasted pre-contract expenditure incurred 'in reasonable contemplation of the contract being entered into,' including expenditure before the contract was signed (e.g., scouting locations, hiring production staff). Lord Denning MR held the key principle: pre-contract reliance expenditure is recoverable if it was within the contemplation of the parties that such expenditure would be wasted on breach.

**McRae v Commonwealth Disposals Commission (1951) 84 CLR 377:** The Commission invited tenders to salvage a tanker said to be stranded on a reef. It did not exist — there was no tanker. The claimant incurred substantial salvage expedition costs. The High Court of Australia awarded reliance damages — the cost of the expedition. The claimant could not prove expectation loss (what profit a non-existent tanker would have yielded), so reliance damages were the appropriate alternative. The case also established there could be a valid contract despite the subject matter's non-existence.

**In short:** Reliance damages are an alternative when expectation loss is too speculative to prove. The claimant recovers wasted expenditure — costs thrown away by the defendant's breach. *Anglia Television* extends this to pre-contract expenditure; *McRae* shows it applies even where the subject matter does not exist.
oscoaReferences: Anglia Television Ltd v Reed [1972] 1 QB 60
McRae v Commonwealth Disposals Commission (1951) 84 CLR 377

question: In __________ Television Ltd v Reed [1972] 1 QB 60, the Court of Appeal awarded reliance damages for wasted pre-contract expenditure incurred in contemplation of the contract.
feedback: Correct. *Anglia Television Ltd v Reed* [1972] 1 QB 60 — the actor Robert Reed repudiated a contract to appear in a TV film. Anglia could not prove expectation loss (the film's profit was speculative). The court awarded all wasted expenditure, including pre-contract expenses, as reliance damages.
oscoaReferences: Anglia Television Ltd v Reed [1972] 1 QB 60

question: In __________ v Commonwealth Disposals Commission (1951) 84 CLR 377, the High Court of Australia awarded reliance damages for wasted expenditure attempting to salvage a tanker that did not exist.
feedback: Correct. *McRae v Commonwealth Disposals Commission* (1951) 84 CLR 377 — the Commission's tender was for a non-existent tanker. Expectation loss was impossible to prove (no profit from a non-existent vessel). The court awarded reliance damages — the cost of the salvage expedition. The case also established there was a valid contract despite the non-existence of the subject matter.
oscoaReferences: McRae v Commonwealth Disposals Commission (1951) 84 CLR 377

question: A restaurant owner contracts with a celebrity chef to host a one-off exclusive dinner event. The restaurant spends £15,000 on marketing, £10,000 on specialist kitchen equipment, and £5,000 on non-refundable ingredient deposits — all before the chef is due to arrive. The chef cancels at the last minute. The restaurant cannot prove what profit the event would have made. Applying *Anglia Television v Reed* [1972], what can the restaurant recover?

options.text: Nothing — the restaurant cannot prove lost profits
options.feedback: Incorrect. Where expectation loss is too speculative, the claimant can claim reliance damages as an alternative. *Anglia Television v Reed* [1972] 1 QB 60.

options.text: All wasted expenditure — the £30,000 in marketing, equipment, and deposits — as reliance damages, because expectation loss is too speculative
options.feedback: Correct. Under *Anglia Television Ltd v Reed* [1972] 1 QB 60, the restaurant can elect to claim reliance damages: all expenditure wasted because of the chef's breach. The marketing, equipment, and deposits were all incurred in reasonable reliance on the contract being performed.

options.text: Only the £5,000 ingredient deposit — the rest was not strictly necessary for the contract
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Anglia Television* [1972] allowed recovery of pre-contract expenditure (like scouting locations). The test is whether the expenditure was within the parties' reasonable contemplation as likely to be wasted on breach, not whether it was strictly necessary.

options.text: The restaurant must claim expectation damages — reliance damages are unavailable if any expectation loss can be imagined
options.feedback: Incorrect. The claimant may elect between expectation and reliance damages. Reliance is particularly appropriate where expectation loss is speculative or impossible to prove.

feedback: The restaurant follows the *Anglia Television* [1972] pattern exactly: a key performer cancels, the project becomes unviable, and expectation loss is too speculative. The claimant can elect reliance damages — all expenditure reasonably incurred in reliance on the contract, including pre-contract costs. The restaurant is put back in the position before the contract, recovering its £30,000 wasted outlay.
oscoaReferences: Anglia Television Ltd v Reed [1972] 1 QB 60

question: Match each case to its holding on reliance damages:
answer: Anglia Television v Reed [1972]=Pre-contract expenditure recoverable as reliance damages where expectation loss too speculative; McRae v Commonwealth Disposals [1951]=Reliance damages for wasted salvage expedition where subject matter (tanker) did not exist

options.text: Pre-contract expenditure recoverable as reliance damages where expectation loss too speculative
Reliance damages for wasted salvage expedition where subject matter (tanker) did not exist

feedback: Correct. Both cases illustrate the reliance measure: where expectation loss is too speculative or impossible to prove, wasted expenditure becomes the alternative. *Anglia Television* [1972] extends this to pre-contract expenditure; *McRae* (1951) establishes that a contract can exist and damages can be awarded even where the subject matter is non-existent.
oscoaReferences: Anglia Television Ltd v Reed [1972] 1 QB 60
McRae v Commonwealth Disposals Commission (1951) 84 CLR 377

question: Which TWO of the following correctly describe the reliance interest in contractual damages?

options.text: The reliance interest puts the claimant in the position as if the contract had been performed
options.feedback: Incorrect. That is the *expectation* interest (*Robinson v Harman* (1848)). The reliance interest puts the claimant *back* to the pre-contract position.

options.text: The claimant may elect to claim reliance damages where expectation loss is too difficult or speculative to prove
options.feedback: Correct. *Anglia Television v Reed* [1972] 1 QB 60 — the claimant could not prove the film's profit, so reliance damages for wasted expenditure were awarded instead.

options.text: Reliance damages include all expenditure, regardless of whether it was reasonably incurred
options.feedback: Incorrect. The expenditure must have been reasonably incurred in reliance on the contract. Reckless or unreasonable expenditure is not recoverable. The defendant's breach must be the effective cause of the waste.

options.text: Pre-contract expenditure may be recoverable as reliance damages if it was within the parties' reasonable contemplation
options.feedback: Correct. *Anglia Television v Reed* [1972] 1 QB 60 — Lord Denning MR held that expenditure incurred before the contract was signed (such as hiring production staff and scouting locations) was recoverable because both parties contemplated that such expenditure would be wasted if the actor withdrew.

options.text: A claimant can claim both expectation and reliance damages for the same breach
options.feedback: Incorrect. The claimant must elect — claiming both would result in double recovery. The claimant chooses the measure that best reflects their loss.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (d). Reliance damages are an alternative where expectation loss is speculative. *Anglia Television* [1972] is the key authority for pre-contract expenditure. The claimant gets back the costs thrown away — they are put in the position *before* the contract, not *after* performance.
oscoaReferences: Anglia Television Ltd v Reed [1972] 1 QB 60
McRae v Commonwealth Disposals Commission (1951) 84 CLR 377

question:
Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'Reliance damages are always calculated as the contract price minus the value of work actually performed. A claimant who has incurred expenditure preparing to perform cannot recover that expenditure if the defendant proves that the contract was a bad bargain for the claimant — the claimant would have made a loss even if the contract had been fully performed.'
answer: The first sentence describes a different measure (quantum meruit), not reliance damages. Reliance damages = wasted expenditure. The second point: while *CCC Films v Impact Quadrant* [1985] QB 16 held that a defendant may prove the contract was a bad bargain to limit reliance damages, the burden of proving this is on the *defendant*. The default assumption is that the claimant would at least have recouped their expenditure.
feedback: Found it. The first sentence describes quantum meruit, not reliance. Reliance damages = wasted expenditure, not 'contract price minus value of work.' The second point: while *CCC Films (London) Ltd v Impact Quadrant Films Ltd* [1985] QB 16 held that a defendant may prove the contract was a bad bargain to limit reliance damages (because the claimant should not be put in a better position than if the contract had been performed), the burden of proving this is on the *defendant*, not the claimant. The default assumption favours the claimant.
oscoaReferences: CCC Films (London) Ltd v Impact Quadrant Films Ltd [1985] QB 16
Anglia Television Ltd v Reed [1972] 1 QB 60

question: The principle that a defendant may prove the contract was a bad bargain to limit reliance damages — but bears the burden of proof — was established in __________ Films v Impact Quadrant Films [1985] QB 16.
feedback: Correct. *CCC Films (London) Ltd v Impact Quadrant Films Ltd* [1985] QB 16 — the defendant argued the claimant would have made a loss even if the contract was performed. Hutchison J held that the *defendant* bears the onus of proving this. Unless the defendant proves the bad bargain, reliance damages are awarded in full.
oscoaReferences: CCC Films (London) Ltd v Impact Quadrant Films Ltd [1985] QB 16

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule — Wrotham Park Damages and Account of Profits:** In exceptional circumstances, the court may move beyond compensatory damages. Two key remedies exist: (1) **negotiating damages** (*Wrotham Park*), which compensate the claimant for the economic value of the right breached — what the claimant could have charged to release the restriction; and (2) **account of profits** (*Blake*), a restitutionary remedy stripping the defendant of profits gained from the breach.

**Wrotham Park Estate Co Ltd v Parkside Homes Ltd [1974] 1 WLR 798:** The defendant built houses in breach of a restrictive covenant. The claimant could not obtain an injunction (houses were already built and occupied) and had suffered no diminution in land value. Brightman J awarded damages assessed at the *hypothetical negotiation* price — what the claimant could reasonably have demanded to release the covenant. These are known as *Wrotham Park* damages or **negotiating damages**.

**Attorney General v Blake [2001] 1 AC 268:** George Blake, a former intelligence officer, breached his lifelong confidentiality undertaking by publishing an autobiography. The Crown suffered no financial loss and could not obtain an injunction (the book was already published). The House of Lords exceptionally awarded an **account of profits** — requiring Blake to disgorge his royalties. Lord Nicholls emphasised this remedy is available only where ordinary remedies (damages, injunctions) are inadequate and there is a special justification, such as a near-fiduciary relationship.

**Morris-Garner v One Step (Support) Ltd [2018] UKSC 20:** The Supreme Court clarified that *Wrotham Park* / negotiating damages are **compensatory**, not restitutionary. They compensate the claimant for the loss of the economic value of the right breached — the price the claimant could have charged to relax the restriction. Lord Reed emphasised that negotiating damages are appropriate where the claimant's loss is 'the economic value of the right which has been breached' — typically in cases involving property rights, restrictive covenants, or confidentiality. The remedy is not available in every case; a legitimate interest in preventing the defendant's activity is required.

**In short:** Negotiating damages (*Wrotham Park*) are compensatory — they value the claimant's lost right. Account of profits (*Blake*) is restitutionary — it strips the defendant's gain. *Morris-Garner* [2018] is the definitive modern statement.
oscoaReferences: Attorney General v Blake [2001] 1 AC 268
Wrotham Park Estate Co Ltd v Parkside Homes Ltd [1974] 1 WLR 798
Morris-Garner v One Step (Support) Ltd [2018] UKSC 20

question: In Attorney General v __________ [2001] 1 AC 268, the House of Lords exceptionally awarded an account of profits for breach of contract where ordinary remedies were inadequate.
feedback: Correct. *Attorney General v Blake* [2001] 1 AC 268 — Blake, a former spy, breached his confidentiality undertaking by publishing memoirs. The Crown suffered no financial loss and could not get an injunction. The exceptional circumstances — near-fiduciary duty, public interest, inadequacy of other remedies — justified ordering an account of profits.
oscoaReferences: Attorney General v Blake [2001] 1 AC 268

question: In __________ Estate Co Ltd v Parkside Homes Ltd [1974] 1 WLR 798, the court awarded damages assessed at the hypothetical negotiation price for release of a restrictive covenant.
feedback: Correct. *Wrotham Park Estate Co Ltd v Parkside Homes Ltd* [1974] 1 WLR 798 — Brightman J awarded damages equal to what the claimant could reasonably have demanded to release the covenant. This is the origin of 'negotiating damages' (also called *Wrotham Park* damages).
oscoaReferences: Wrotham Park Estate Co Ltd v Parkside Homes Ltd [1974] 1 WLR 798

question: In __________ v One Step (Support) Ltd [2018] UKSC 20, the Supreme Court held that Wrotham Park / negotiating damages are compensatory in nature, not restitutionary.
feedback: Correct. *Morris-Garner v One Step (Support) Ltd* [2018] UKSC 20 — Lord Reed clarified that negotiating damages compensate the claimant for the economic value of the right breached (the price of a hypothetical release). They are not restitutionary — they do not disgorge the defendant's profits, unlike the account of profits in *Blake* [2001].
oscoaReferences: Morris-Garner v One Step (Support) Ltd [2018] UKSC 20

question: A pharmaceutical researcher, under a lifelong confidentiality clause, leaves her employer and writes a bestselling memoir revealing trade secrets — earning £2 million in royalties. The employer suffered no financial loss (the secrets were not commercially exploited by the competitor) and an injunction is now impossible. Applying *Attorney General v Blake* [2001]:

options.text: The employer can recover only nominal damages — no financial loss was suffered
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Blake* [2001] was precisely a case where the Crown suffered no financial loss, but the House of Lords exceptionally awarded an account of profits. Ordinary remedies being inadequate is the trigger for the exceptional remedy.

options.text: The employer is entitled to £2 million as expectation damages — putting the employer where it would have been had the contract been performed
options.feedback: Incorrect. The employer cannot prove expectation loss — it suffered no quantifiable financial loss. Expectation damages would be nil or nominal. The claim is properly framed as a restitutionary remedy.

options.text: The employer may seek an account of profits — the facts closely mirror *Blake*: a lifelong confidentiality obligation, no financial loss, and ordinary remedies are inadequate
options.feedback: Correct. The facts are directly analogous to *Attorney General v Blake* [2001] 1 AC 268. The exceptional circumstances — the confidential relationship (near-fiduciary), the inadequacy of ordinary remedies, and the deliberate nature of the breach — justify an account of profits. The employer could claim the £2 million in royalties.

options.text: The employer can only obtain an injunction — damages are never available for breach of a confidentiality clause
options.feedback: Incorrect. Damages are always available for breach of contract. The issue is the *measure* of damages. An injunction is also unavailable because the book has already been published.

feedback: This scenario maps directly onto *Attorney General v Blake* [2001] 1 AC 268. Lord Nicholls identified the key factors: (1) a near-fiduciary relationship, (2) ordinary remedies being inadequate (no financial loss; injunction impossible), (3) the defendant's deliberate and profit-making breach. The account of profits disgorges the £2 million. Note: this remedy remains exceptional and will not be granted in ordinary commercial disputes.
oscoaReferences: Attorney General v Blake [2001] 1 AC 268

question: Match each remedy to the correct case and description:
answer: Account of profits=Attorney General v Blake [2001] — disgorge defendant's profits, restitutionary; Negotiating damages=Wrotham Park v Parkside Homes [1974] — hypothetical release price, compensatory; Loss of amenity=Ruxley v Forsyth [1996] — non-pecuniary loss, compensatory

options.text: Attorney General v Blake [2001] — disgorge defendant's profits, restitutionary
Wrotham Park v Parkside Homes [1974] — hypothetical release price, compensatory
Ruxley v Forsyth [1996] — non-pecuniary loss, compensatory

feedback: Correct. Account of profits (*Blake*) is restitutionary (defendant-focused); negotiating damages (*Wrotham Park*) and loss of amenity (*Ruxley*) are compensatory (claimant-focused). *Morris-Garner* [2018] confirmed negotiating damages are compensatory — they value the claimant's lost right, not the defendant's gain.
oscoaReferences: Attorney General v Blake [2001] 1 AC 268
Wrotham Park Estate Co Ltd v Parkside Homes Ltd [1974] 1 WLR 798
Ruxley Electronics and Construction Ltd v Forsyth [1996] AC 344
Morris-Garner v One Step (Support) Ltd [2018] UKSC 20

question: Which TWO of the following correctly describe the remedy of Wrotham Park / negotiating damages as clarified in *Morris-Garner v One Step* [2018]?

options.text: Wrotham Park damages are restitutionary — they disgorge the defendant's profits from the breach
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Morris-Garner v One Step* [2018] UKSC 20 confirmed that negotiating damages are *compensatory*, not restitutionary. Account of profits (*Blake* [2001]) is the restitutionary remedy.

options.text: Negotiating damages compensate the claimant for the economic value of the right that has been breached
options.feedback: Correct. Lord Reed in *Morris-Garner v One Step* [2018] UKSC 20 stated that negotiating damages are awarded where the loss is 'the economic value of the right which has been breached.' They reflect what the claimant could have charged to release the restriction.

options.text: Wrotham Park damages are appropriate where the claimant has suffered no financial loss but has been deprived of a valuable bargaining right
options.feedback: Correct. In *Wrotham Park* [1974] itself, the claimant's land had not diminished in value, but it had lost the right to control development — a valuable bargaining chip. Negotiating damages value that lost bargaining right at the hypothetical release price.

options.text: Wrotham Park damages are available in all cases of breach of contract as an alternative to expectation damages
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Morris-Garner* [2018] emphasised that negotiating damages are not a universal remedy. They are available where the nature of the breach makes it appropriate to value the loss at the hypothetical release price — typically property rights, restrictive covenants, or confidentiality cases.

options.text: Negotiating damages are always calculated as a proportion of the defendant's profits
options.feedback: Incorrect. Negotiating damages are based on the *claimant's* hypothetical bargaining position, not the defendant's actual profits. They ask: what would the claimant have charged to release the covenant? The defendant's profit is a separate inquiry (account of profits).

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (c). *Morris-Garner v One Step (Support) Ltd* [2018] UKSC 20 is now the leading authority on negotiating damages. The key distinction: negotiating damages are compensatory (valuing the claimant's lost right), while account of profits is restitutionary (stripping the defendant's gain). *Wrotham Park* [1974] remains good law as clarified by the Supreme Court.
oscoaReferences: Morris-Garner v One Step (Support) Ltd [2018] UKSC 20
Wrotham Park Estate Co Ltd v Parkside Homes Ltd [1974] 1 WLR 798
Attorney General v Blake [2001] 1 AC 268

question:
Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'After *Attorney General v Blake* [2001], an account of profits is available in any commercial contract dispute where the defendant has made a profit from breaching the contract. The claimant can always choose between expectation damages and an account of profits, whichever is higher.'
answer: Account of profits is an *exceptional* remedy, not generally available. *Blake* [2001] stressed it is only available where ordinary remedies are inadequate and there is a special justification — in Blake itself, a near-fiduciary confidentiality relationship. In ordinary commercial contracts, the remedy is not available. Claimants cannot simply elect whichever remedy is highest.
feedback: Found it. Lord Nicholls in *Attorney General v Blake* [2001] 1 AC 268 was at pains to emphasise that account of profits is exceptional — not available in ordinary commercial disputes. It requires special circumstances: a near-fiduciary relationship, inadequacy of ordinary remedies, and a legitimate interest in preventing the defendant's profit-making activity. In *Experience Hendrix LLC v PPX Enterprises Inc* [2003] EWCA Civ 323, the Court of Appeal declined to award an account of profits, awarding negotiating damages instead.
oscoaReferences: Attorney General v Blake [2001] 1 AC 268
Experience Hendrix LLC v PPX Enterprises Inc [2003] EWCA Civ 323

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block A] A shop contracts with a supplier for 1,000 branded T-shirts at £5 each for a summer event. The supplier fails to deliver. The shop buys replacement T-shirts at £8 each on the open market. The shop sells all 1,000 at £15 each. Applying *Robinson v Harman* (1848), what is the shop's expectation loss?

options.text: £3,000 — the extra purchase cost (1,000 x (£8 - £5)) representing the net diminution in profit
options.feedback: Correct. Under *Robinson v Harman* (1848) 1 Ex 850: position with performance = buy at £5, sell at £15 = £10,000 profit. Actual position = buy at £8, sell at £15 = £7,000 profit. Expectation loss = £10,000 - £7,000 = £3,000. The £3,000 is precisely the extra purchase cost: 1,000 x (£8 - £5).

options.text: £5,000 — the original contract price
options.feedback: Incorrect. The full contract price would overcompensate — the shop would have paid this anyway. Expectation damages are the *net* loss.

options.text: £10,000 — the lost gross profit (1,000 x (£15 - £5))
options.feedback: Incorrect. This ignores the fact that the shop could and did buy replacements and still made a profit. The correct measure accounts for mitigation — the shop actually bought at £8 and sold at £15.

options.text: £7,000 — the actual profit the shop made
options.feedback: Incorrect. The shop's £7,000 profit is not the loss — it is the residual position. The loss is the difference between what profit should have been (£10,000) and what it was (£7,000) = £3,000.

feedback: The expectation measure: the shop ends up where it would have been with performance — net of mitigation. The shop bought replacements and still sold them profitably, so the loss is only the extra purchase cost (£3,000). This reflects the principle in *Robinson v Harman* (1848) 1 Ex 850 — the claimant is compensated for the net shortfall.
oscoaReferences: Robinson v Harman (1848) 1 Ex 850

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block A] In __________ Electronics v Forsyth [1996] AC 344, the House of Lords held that the cost of cure must be reasonable and proportionate, and awarded loss of amenity damages instead.
feedback: Correct. *Ruxley Electronics and Construction Ltd v Forsyth* [1996] AC 344 — a swimming pool built 6 inches too shallow. Cost of cure £21,560; diminution in value nil. The court instead awarded £2,500 for loss of amenity. This consolidates Block A: cost of cure is not automatic; proportionality matters.
oscoaReferences: Ruxley Electronics and Construction Ltd v Forsyth [1996] AC 344

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Blocks B & C] Which TWO of the following correctly distinguish reliance damages from accounts of profits?

options.text: Reliance damages compensate the claimant for wasted expenditure; an account of profits strips the defendant of gains made from the breach
options.feedback: Correct. This is the fundamental distinction. Reliance is claimant-focused (back to pre-contract position); account of profits is defendant-focused (disgorgement). *Anglia Television* [1972] and *Blake* [2001] respectively.

options.text: Both are available as of right in any breach of contract claim
options.feedback: Incorrect. Reliance is an alternative to expectation (where expectation is too speculative). Account of profits is an exceptional remedy — only where ordinary remedies are inadequate (*Blake* [2001]).

options.text: Reliance damages are always larger than an account of profits
options.feedback: Incorrect. The size depends on the facts. In *Blake* [2001], the account of profits (£90,000+ in royalties) far exceeded the Crown's reliance loss (nil). The measures are conceptually different; either could be larger.

options.text: An account of profits is the standard remedy in construction contract disputes
options.feedback: Incorrect. Account of profits is exceptional — it has never been awarded in an ordinary commercial or construction dispute. Expectation damages or cost of cure (*Ruxley* [1996]) are the standard measures.

options.text: Reliance damages put the claimant back to the pre-contract position; an account of profits ensures the defendant does not benefit from wrongdoing
options.feedback: Correct. The reliance interest is backward-looking (undoing the claimant's detrimental reliance); account of profits is forward-looking from the defendant's perspective (disgorging unjust enrichment). Two distinct rationales: compensation vs restitution.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (e). Reliance damages = wasted expenditure (compensatory, claimant-focused, backward-looking). Account of profits = disgorgement of defendant's gains (restitutionary, defendant-focused, forward-looking). Both are alternatives to expectation damages, but for different reasons and in different circumstances.
oscoaReferences: Anglia Television Ltd v Reed [1972] 1 QB 60
Attorney General v Blake [2001] 1 AC 268

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block C] A developer builds a block of flats that encroaches by 2 metres onto a neighbour's land, in breach of a restrictive covenant. The neighbour's land value is unaffected (£500,000 before and after). The neighbour cannot get an injunction because the flats are already built and occupied. The developer made a £2 million profit on the flats. Applying *Wrotham Park* [1974] and *Morris-Garner* [2018], what is the neighbour's likely remedy?

options.text: £2 million — an account of profits under *Blake* [2001]
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Blake* [2001] is not available in ordinary land disputes. The developer is not in a near-fiduciary position. The account of profits is confined to exceptional cases. *Experience Hendrix* [2003] confirmed this distinction.

options.text: Negotiating damages under *Wrotham Park* — the hypothetical price the neighbour could have demanded to release the covenant, assessed at its economic value
options.feedback: Correct. This mirrors *Wrotham Park Estate Co Ltd v Parkside Homes Ltd* [1974] 1 WLR 798. The neighbour has suffered no diminution in value, but has lost a valuable bargaining right. Negotiating damages compensate for the economic value of that right — the price a reasonable person in the neighbour's position would have demanded. Under *Morris-Garner* [2018] UKSC 20, this is compensatory.

options.text: Nominal damages only — the land value is unaffected
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Wrotham Park* [1974] was exactly a case where land value was unaffected but negotiating damages were awarded. A claimant who has lost a bargaining right has suffered a real economic loss, even if the physical asset value is unchanged.

options.text: Expectation damages equal to the developer's £2 million profit
options.feedback: Incorrect. The neighbour's expectation loss (the position with performance — no encroachment) would be nil or unable to be proved. £2 million is the developer's gain, not the neighbour's loss. This is a negotiating damages scenario, not expectation.

feedback: This consolidates Block C. The facts are directly on point with *Wrotham Park* [1974]: no diminution in land value, no injunction possible, but a valuable right has been breached. *Morris-Garner v One Step* [2018] UKSC 20 confirmed the compensatory nature of negotiating damages. The neighbour recovers the hypothetical release fee — the economic value of the right to say 'no' to the encroachment. The developer's £2 million profit is relevant to the negotiation, but the measure is the neighbour's loss, not the developer's gain.
oscoaReferences: Wrotham Park Estate Co Ltd v Parkside Homes Ltd [1974] 1 WLR 798
Morris-Garner v One Step (Support) Ltd [2018] UKSC 20
Experience Hendrix LLC v PPX Enterprises Inc [2003] EWCA Civ 323

question: [CONSOLIDATION — All Blocks] The primary measure of contractual damages — the __________ interest — was formulated in *Robinson v Harman* (1848) 1 Ex 850 as putting the claimant in the position as if the contract had been performed.
feedback: Correct. The expectation interest is the default and primary measure. Parke B in *Robinson v Harman* (1848) 1 Ex 850: 'where a party sustains a loss by reason of a breach of contract, he is, so far as money can do it, to be placed in the same situation… as if the contract had been performed.' This unifying principle underpins all damages measures explored in this lesson.
oscoaReferences: Robinson v Harman (1848) 1 Ex 850
